res00061.title: Introduction
res00061.content:
Thanks for choosing North Metropolitan TAFE as your training provider.

You can use the menu on the left to navigate through this unit.

  • Learning Materials - In this section you will find all of the learning resources required for this unit.
  • You will find details of all of the assessments in the Assessment section
  • My Grades is where you will find your assessment feedback once marked
  • Blackboard Collaborate is an online Classroom where your lecturer may choose to meet or hold sessions
  • In the Communication section you will find tools to contact your lecturer or classmates
  • If you have any questions or problems, please see the Staff contacts section


This unit describes the performance outcome, knowledge and skills required to recognise threats, risks and vulnerabilities to cyber security in an organisation.

The threats to an organisation include networks, machines, applications, data, users and infrastructure.

In your study, you will investigate common cyber security attack mechanisms and an introduction to threat management as well as security issues surrounding Internet of Things (IoT) devices.

You will also investigate the implementation of tools and systems an organisation can use for protection against cyber-attacks.

describe what you will be assessed on.

res00073.title: Session 1

Course Introduction and Overview
  • Cisco Cybersecurity Essential
  • Reasons to protect personal and organisation’s data

res00095.title: Week 1
res00095.content:
What this course is all about
This unit deals with the performance outcome, knowledge and skills required to recognise threats, risks and vulnerabilities to cyber security in an organisation.
The threats to an organisation include networks, machines, applications, data, users and infrastructure.
Common cyber security attack mechanisms and an introduction to threat management as well as security issues surrounding Internet of Things (IoT) devices are addressed.
The implementation of tools and systems an organisation can use for protection against cyber-attacks are also investigated.
(Images - CSIRO)


Day 1 - Step by Step
Your lecturer will introduce expectations of subject.
Together you will go through the Unit Learning & Assessment Plan (found under "Welcome") and analyse assessment requirements.
It is important that you listen to the lecturer's expectations for each assessment submission.
You have five TASKS or labs which are Required as part of the Assessment 1 Portfolio of Tasks.
An important first principle of security - There is no such thing as absolute security.
Consider the implications of IBM predictions of quantum computers (see link in Slide 4 of the PowerPoint below)
In Session Activity:
  1. Recorgnise the need for cyber security. Poor security led to pathology hack and prosecution | Information Age | ACS -
    Poor security led to pathology hack and prosecution.docx
  2. Similar story, different regulator prosecuting for poor cyber security, ASIC sues company over prolonged cybersecurity failures | Information Age | ACS
  3. Even large Aussi government agencies need to improve their cyber security Govt agencies unprepared for ‘significant’ cyber incident | Information Age | ACS
  4. What does Cyber security involve? View Cyber Security Body of Knowledge (CyBoK)
  5. Enrol in Cisco Cybersecurity Essentials. Have a look at the Course Index noting what is in each of the 8 chapters.
    Also note this Recognise the Need for Cyber Security unit does NOT include all the Cybersecurity Essentials chapters nor are you expected or required to complete the Cybersecurity Essentials assessments.
    Enrolling using the Seat Token.docx
    See Out of Session Activities below for first session reading and actions.
Important Learnings
  1. Security Principles: CIA Triad - examine the definitions of Confidentiality, Integrity and Availability and how they apply as concepts to Information Security.
  2. CISCO Cybersecrity Essentials Readings and actions:
    • Attempt Chapter 1 Quiz before reading any of the Chapter. Note what you did and didn't know to answer
    • Active Reading: 1.2.1 Cybersecurity Criminals (3 pages)
    • Active Reading: 1.3.1 Threat Arenas (6 pages)
    • Attempt Chapter 2 Quiz before reading any of the Chapter. Note what you did and didn't know to answer
    • Active Reading: 2.0 Introduction (1 page)
    • Active Reading: 2.1 (3 pages)
Out of Session Activities:
  • Complete the Cybersecurity Essentials Chapter 1 and 2 readings listed above.
  • Complete the Cisco activity "1.5.1.3 Activity - Identify the NIST/NICE Cybersecurity Specialty Areas"

    Further Discussion questions (If no time in session, read at home. Discuss next week):
  • How have privacy laws changes since the Universal Declaration of Human Rights : Article 12 Right to Privacy (1948)?
  • What effect are IOT and AI having on data protection risk?
If interested in how bots work view this video by CGP Grey How bots work - CGP Grey
If interested in quantum computing, start with this, 2024 Quantum Computing Update - Explaining Computers - YouTube (15 minutes)

This article from mid July gives further reasons why quantum computing is a worry for cyber security people.
https://ia.acs.org.au/content/ia/article/2021/quantum-decryption-coming.html?ref=newsletter

res00097.title: Session 4
res00097.content:
News

1. ‘Undercooked’: Scam reforms rushed through Parliament | Information Age | ACS

2. ThreatWire 25 Jan 2024 | Patreon watch until end of 3rd story - 4 min total
Threatwire a weekly video podcast which has a number of stories each week on hacking and other cyber security related events. Although USA based, it is still a good source of the nature of current threats and the failures that allowed them to succeed.
This weeks stories are

  1. USA Security and Exchange Commission (SEC) X-account hacked via a SIM swapping attack and because its 2FA was switched off
  2. USA IT pen tester gets found guilty and fined for unauthorised penetration testing despite uncovering a very serious vulnerability of the target company
  3. China uni develops a way of revealing the senders, recipients and contents of Airdrop (Apple to Apple wireless file transfer method)

3. Should I give my banking password to a mortgage broker? | Information Age | ACS - "Screen scraping" Vs Open Banking with Consumer Data Rights (CDR)

4. Cyber company duped by North Korean hacker | Information Age | ACS - State sponsored threat actor tricked the usual vetting procedures when hired.

5. China-based backdoor found in health devices | Information Age | ACS - Medical IoT device

Session Topic:
During the session we Investigate common and emerging cyber security attacks, and techniques.
Specifically we will be investigating:
  • The known Cyber attacks identified and characterised
  • How they work, what vulnerabilities are exploited.

Then we will examine the Internet of Things (IoT);

  • IoT devices are presented
  • The reasons for their deployment (why they are used)
  • The particular problems with ensuring their security, both standalone and on the network.

Session Topic:
During the session we investigate the various methods and techiques employed to protect personal data and privacy
  • Identity and Access Management (IAM, ponounced I am)
  • Protection from cyber threats
  • The known Cyber attacks identified and characterised
  • How they work, what vulnerabilities are exploited.
    Identity and Access Management

    Identity and access management (IAM) is the cybersecurity discipline that deals with how users access digital resources and what they can do with those resources.
    IAM systems keep hackers out while ensuring that each individual user has the exact permissions they need to do their jobs and not more than that.
    This is most relevant to the "C", Confidentiality and to a lesser extent the "I", Integrity of the cyber security principles CIA triad.
    Applying Authetication, Authorization and Accounting (AAA) methods are an important part of IAM.



    Security Essentials - Access Control.pptx

    Access Control Models (Professor Messer, youtube.com)

    Access Control Technologies (Professor Messer, youtube.com)

    Note: These access control models are also discussed in the Security Fundamentals Session 12.

    Portfolio Tasks:

    • Setup Windows client machine
    • Configure networking elements.
    • Capture packets with Wireshark using Telnet and SSH

News

  3. Here’s how a JavaScript Supply Chain Attack works:

    • Step 1: A 3rd party JavaScript library is compromised
    • Step 2: Customer visits the webpage like the payment page with the compromised JS file
    • Step 3: Data is exfiltrated one record (credit card number for example) at a time

    100K websites were compromised using this tactic with the pollyfill.io JS hack last month (July 2024).

Topic

We will investigate common infrastructure, equipment, and and software that is employed to protect the organisation from cyber security attacks.

res00100.title: Session 8
res00100.content:
Introduction

During this session we will be investigating the frameworks used to help organisations protect against cyber attacks.



News
  1. More bad news about the Cyber security professionals - looks like original report was from the UK, but reflects Australian concerns.
    Cyber specialists moonlighting as dark web criminals | Information Age | ACS

  2. This next story is an update of the Microsoft Attack story from Session 3
    Hackers lurk in Microsoft systems for 3 months | Information Age | ACS

  3. Notre Dame uni students say outage creating enrolment, graduation, assignment mayhem - ABC News

  4. (not News, but a look at verious Cyber Tools) Download | Hiren's BootCD PE


Topic
This session you will examine methods used to protect an organisation’s data.
The main focus will be on the following:
  • MITRE adversarial tactics
  • Techniques and Common Knowledge (ATT&CK)

MITRE ATT&CK (Adversarial Tactics, Techniques and Common Knowledge) is a framework, set of data matrices, and assessment tool developed by MITRE Corporation to help organizations understand their security readiness and uncover vulnerabilities in their defenses.

Out of Session Activity
Review the Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain and the Mitre ATT&CK framework.
Consider how they are related.
Importantly, the Cyber Kill Chain has a military origin with external threats.



Portfolio Tasks

Lastly, Known attackers have been organized into groups including their history of attacks and the techniques they use. Groups | MITRE ATT&CK®

res00101.title: Session 9
res00101.content:
Introduction

During this session we will be examining various ways of protecting an organisation's data.
Typically the organisation's data is one of the most valuable assets owned by the organisation.
Protection includes preventing theft, unauthorised access, and unauthorised changes or corruption.


News
  1. Why can’t I get a graduate job in cyber security? | Information Age | ACS
    Does this apply to you as a potential TAFE graduate? DISCUSS with the lecturer!

  2. Paying cyber-ransoms still lands organisations in hot water - Australian Cyber Security Magazine

  3. New National Cyber Security Coordinator announced - Australian Cyber Security Magazine
Topic
During this session we will continue to examine methods used to protect an organisation's data.
We will consider various policies, tools and systems for cyber protection.
These include
  • Host Intrusion Detection Systems,
  • Cryptography, and
  • various means of obfuscating data
Out of Session Activity
Review the various methods for protecting data in your own personal or organisational use.
Think about how these compare to what was discussed during this session.

Complete outstanding work

res00116.title: Presentation
res00116.content:
This is presentation for Session 9

HIDS Vs NIDS

What Is Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) and How It Works

Cyber Security Policy
Create a cyber security policy | business.gov.au

Cyber Security Procedures and Protocols include the following instruction steps:

  1. Identify the assets that need protection.
  2. Protect the assets by implementing security measures.
  3. Monitor the system for any security threats.
  4. Respond to incidents promptly.
  5. Recover from any cybersecurity breaches.

Note: Procedures are usually formally formatted as a series of numbered steps to be followed.
Dynamic playbooks contain these procedures are are used to direct users, depending on their pre-defined roles how to response to specific incidences.
The "dynamic" part means there are a choice to make from the number of "next steps" options to take depending on the results of the previous step.

res00123.title: Presentation
res00123.content:
This is the presentation for Session 10

The process for an organization to obtaining a ISO27001:2022 compliance certification is set out in the following document
27001 Cheat Sheet.pdf Open and read please!
Notes:

  1. There is a 2022 update to ISO27001 with organizations expected to comply with the updated standard by October 2025.
  2. Apart from a successful checklist/audits/certification, the actual output of the ISO27001 process is a customised Information Security Management System (ISMS) for that organization.
    The ISMS consists of a complete set of cyber security policy and procedure documents.
    These must be regularly reviewed and updated to keep ISO27001 compliance.

Topic
Managing the cyber security of any sized organisation is generally a daunting prospect.
Each organisation has different requirements and presents different vulnerabilities - even when the organisations in question may have similar network infrastructures.
In order to deal with these challenges, various frameworks have been developed to assist the organisation's cyber defence personnel.
During this session we will investigate the current Cyber Security Frameworks (CSF), focussing on:
  • ISO 27000 series (see Session 8)
  • NIST
  • Essential Eight


Out of Session Activity

res00106.title: Session 14
res00106.content:
Introduction

The CIS (Center for Internet Security) Critical Security Controls are a prioritized set of actions for cybersecurity.
Thes controls form a defense-in-depth set of specific and actionable best practices to manage and deal with the most common cyber attacks
During this session we will examine the CIS controls



News
  1. More Aussi Telcos in trouble, Dodo, iPrimus data breach sees email and SIM cards hacked | Information Age | ACS

  2. Global outage affecting Australia. AWS outage knocks out Netflix, YouTube, Roblox | Information Age | ACS

  3. Ongoing repercussions of a breach. Medibank 2nd Class Action


    Medibank faces second class action over Russian hacker data theft

    The health records of almost 10 million Australians were stolen in the attack, then dumped on the dark web.

  4. To followup the news article in Session 9, How to get a job in cyber security | Information Age | ACS .

    When you are nearing the end of your study, make you have a good read of this. ACS-CareerGuide_Cybersecurity.pdf
  5. Not exactly news, but this might be of interest to some of you, Simplilearn AI in CyberSecurity.pptx


Topic
This is our last formal session for Security Essentials.
We will conclude our investigation into the current cyber security frameworks (CSF).
Specifically we will focus our attention on the Centre for Internet Security (CIS) controls for cyber security protection.


Out of Session Activity
Review the CIS controls, and think about how this framework compares to the others studied:
  • Cyber kill chain
  • Essential 8
  • NIST
Read the article on the Medibank legal woes.
Now that we can look back on this, is there anything obvious in the cyber defence that should improved on?


Portfolio Tasks

Complete and submit AT1 (Portfolio) PART-FIVE Essentials

res00136.title: Assessment Overview
res00136.content:
All assessments for the unit or cluster can be accessed here and are required to be completed by the due dates as stated in the Learning and Assessment Plan.

Submission Warning

Once you have completed your assessment and wish to submit it you MUST be aware that when you click the " Submit" button you are formally declaring that the attached work is your own. Do not click " Submit" if this is not the case, and do not allow others to access your Blackboard account as you will be held accountable for anything submitted using your login details.

When completing any submission you are acknowledging the following:

a. The attached submission is completely my own work
b. I have correctly cited all sources of information used in this work (if required)
c. I have kept a copy of this assessment (where practicable)
d. I understand a copy of my assessment will be kept by the NMTAFE for their records
e. I understand my assessment may be selected for use in the NMTAFE’s validation and audit process to ensure student assessment meets requirements

Results
Results for assessments in this unit/cluster can be viewed by going to My Grades in the left hand menu. If you have any queries regarding your assessment results, comments and suggestions, please contact your lecturer. If we do not hear from you, we will assume your acknowledgement and acceptance of the feedback.

res00137.title: How to submit your work

res00139.title: How to submit your work

res00146.title: Submission and Documentation Instructions
res00146.content:
You are required to submit all of your documented evidence for the below work, each of the submissions must contain the following or they will be rejected
  1. Activities:
    1. Evidence that you completed each part of the activity, these should be signed screen shots (one option would be the snip tool in Windows)
  2. Packet Tracer Activities:
    1. The completed Packet Tracer file
    2. The completed documentation that accompanies the Packet Tracer file including (open the PDF files in Microsoft Word to edit):
      1. Screen shots of you completing the tasks
      2. Answers to any questions in the document
    3. Any other evidence that you feel will help you prove you completed the work
  3. Lab Activities:
    1. A .txt file for each config of each device (for example if your exercise has 2 routers and a switch you should include 3 files called R1.txt, R2.txt and S1.txt with the copy and pasted running-configuration of the devices in each)
    2. Completed lab documentation including:
      1. Screen shots to demonstrate you completed steps in the labs
      2. Answers to any questions in the document
    3. Any other evidence you feel will help you prove you completed the work
All documentation:
  1. Screen shots of you entering every command required by the document (for example: if the document asks you to configure a router network interface, you must show screen shots to verify you did).
  2. Screen shots to verify your commands are correctly configured. This means if you are configuring networks or routing you must demonstrate that they work, this should be done through testing (ping, debug or show commands will be useful here (most labs will ask you to verify instructions anyway)).
  3. Where appropriate, sign your screenshots, or add a "whoami" insert. Example here:

Any submissions not meeting the above requirements will be rejected. All submissions must be demonstrably, clearly and unquestionably your work; any question of authenticity will result in the rejection of submissions. There is a zero tolerance plagiarism policy enforced.
If you have any questions, please do not hesitate to ask your lecturer either in person or over email (see the contacts section of BlackBoard).

res00151.title: Assessment 1
res00151.content:
Instructions to Students
  1. Answer all the questions.
  2. Put your responses in the spaces provided.
  3. Your responses must be in your own words, and should be a maximum of fifty words in length, or two to three sentences. Q22 may require a longer answer.
  4. Referencing is not applicable, and any cut and paste responses are invalid.
